@xragua, I've just started having a look at the paper and have a few comments, starting with a broad one. Most of the summary (lines 8–34) contains no references at all, which I think needs correcting. I'm not expecting anything hugely detailed but references to a few key review papers, books or high-impact results over the years would be appropriate. Note that there's also a mix of XRAYBINARYORBIT (e.g. [the repo README](https://github.com/xragua/xraybinaryorbit)) and xraybinaryorbit (e.g. [the docs](https://xragua.github.io/xraybinaryorbit/)) as the software's name. Because we don't really support things like small caps, as other journals do, we tend towards having all lowercase names. Note that when I make the editorial corrections (when I put my assistant editor-in-chief hat on), I'll remove all the boldface text anyway. As a smaller point, Fig. 1 has embedded text that I won't be able to propose corrections to via pull request. Note that "Particle swarm" should be "particle swarm", "Real Ionization" should be "Real ionization" and you've written "trayectory" instead of "trajectory" I'd also suggest moving the _Statement of Need_ to follow the _Summary_. This shouldn't affect the flow of the text, and I think the last two paragraphs of the _Summary_ could be places at the end of the _Statement of Need_. I.e., in effect, you could move the _Statement of Need_ to what is [currently ](https://github.com/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/7220#issuecomment-2510245807) the start of page 2 but maybe even line 35. Finally, if any of your co-authors have ORCIDs, you should add them, but I realise not all researchers have them.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2510261092/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

for these changes @xragua, we're getting there! As another small point, the section title "Science Behind" doesn't make sense on its own. Perhaps you want something like "Background theory", "Scientific background" or anything else along those lines. More substantially regarding the image again, the fourth caption should presumably be "Ionization maps of the **orbital** plane...". We also tend not to have so many images in JOSS papers, so I'd suggest removing the second (the fit to the spin period evolution), because it does the least to demonstrate the features of xraybinaryorbit. You should then split each of the remaining panels into their own figure, with the caption in the text rather than embedded in the image. (The images are quite wide so I expect the won't take up that much vertical space on the page.) Finally, you'll need to refer to all three figures in the text. I.e., the last sentence in the *Statement of Need* will have to become > Some results obtained with this package are shown in **Figures 1, 2 and 3**. Splitting up the figures, getting the text into the captions and removing the second fit (the basic curve fit) are the most important things. I can propose changes to the text via a pull request after that, if you have any issues yourself.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2529439256/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

@xragua — I suspect there's a small issue in the names in the metadata. Specifically, the names are currently being interpreted as (boldface indicates **surname**): 1. Graciela **Sanjurjo-Ferrín** 2. Jessica Planelles **Villalva** 3. Jose Miguel **Torrejón** 4. Jose Joaquín **Rodes-Roca** I think (from the [ORCID](https://orcid.org/0009-0007-9988-0202)) author 2 should be *Jessica **Planelles Villalva***. If I've understood correctly, you can indicate the specific components of [names](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/paper.html#names) by replacing ``` - name: Jessica Planelles Villalva ``` with ``` - given-names: Jessica - surname: Planelles Villalva ``` I'll leave this to you to change in case you need to make similar corrections to the other names but I *think* they've been automatically interpreted correctly. If you let me know when it's fixed, I'll do another test run of the publication pipeline. Thanks for your patience! @ConnorStoneAstro, I'm the AEiC for this JOSS "track". Before anything else, I need to flag that the paper is currently too long. Specifically, from the [JOSS docs](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/paper.html#what-should-my-paper-contain) (emphasis mine), > The paper should be between **250-1000 words**. Authors submitting papers significantly longer than 1000 words may be asked to reduce the length of their paper. I apologise that we should've flagged this earlier and understand we might not get all the way down to 1000 words but the article is currently around 3600 words. Note the short list of items that need to be in the paper, in the same docs linked above. At a glance, I'll recommend the following: * The "Performance" section can go, though you may wish to mention the performance relative to other packages (like lenstronomy) and the GPU performance in a few sentences under statement of need. * The "User experience" section can go entirely. Our docs specifically say > Given this format, a “full length” paper is not permitted, and software documentation such as API (Application Programming Interface) functionality should not be in the paper and instead should be outlined in the software documentation. * Similarly, the "Flexibility", "Machine Learning" and "Conclusions" don't warrant whole sections. Machine learning is already mentioned sufficiently in the "Scope" section. Some of the "Conclusions" text might be consolidated into the summary. You're welcome to transplant any of this material to your docs. Let me know if you have any questions! If you'd like to run the word count again, you can comment with `@editorialbot check repository`. I fully acknowledge that words counts are a bit uncertain but I'd at least like to get this under 2000 words, if not closer to 1000.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2479808034/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Specifically, I think these lines ``` Copyright (c)[2024 Chun Huang, João Cartaxo, Tuhin Malik, Wenli Yuan, Shashwat Sourav, Xuezhi Liu, Tianzhe Zhou, John Groger, Zhiheng Wang, Nicole Osborn, Nathan Whitsett] ``` might need to be ``` Copyright (c) 2024 Chun Huang, João Cartaxo, Tuhin Malik, Wenli Yuan, Shashwat Sourav, Xuezhi Liu, Tianzhe Zhou, John Groger, Zhiheng Wang, Nicole Osborn, Nathan Whitsett ``` and you might even need to put all the authors back on one line. Have a look at other MIT Licensed code you might know of or browse past JOSS articles to see what might be the issue here, then run `@editorialbot check repository` to rerun our checks. Also, the paper is failing to build at least because of a YAML error in the metadata, which is also flagged by the GitHub UI if you [view the file there](https://github.com/ChunHuangPhy/CompactObject/blob/b5705dceece84acb4c4cb6f40b6ddfb6100d2a84/JOSS/paper.md). All our editors are currently busy so I'm going to place this on our current waiting list. In any case, the project you've submitted has a variety of issues that I've listed below that need to be addressed before we open this up for an editor to pick up, so I'm going to tag it as "paused" until the following points are addressed. 1. The repo description says it's a "test repo of Qhx". Is that still true? I see you have several different Qhx repos, so it isn't clear this is the one you intended to submit. 2. There are a lot of extra files in the repo, including at least * some files named `0`, `1`, `2` and `3`, * a bunch of CSV files, * some Jupyter Notebooks (which might be part of the docs), * probably some profiling output in `prof` and * some build artefacts (e.g. `Qhx.egg-info/` and `build/`). I don't think any of these should be in the repo and are only likely to confuse any potential reviewers. 3. The Qhx documentation describes [two versions](https://lionandjelka.github.io/QhX1/important_note.html) of the same code. A final step in the JOSS publication process will be to tag a new release version, which is difficult to reconcile with having two versions. Regardless, just for ease-of-use, it makes more sense to have a single package with appropriate options—be that different functions or relevant arguments—to select between two different modes of execution. 4. This is less important at the outset but the paper is also a bit long. The [JOSS documentation says](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/paper.html#what-should-my-paper-contain): > The paper should be between 250-1000 words. Authors submitting papers significantly longer than 1000 words may be asked to reduce the length of their paper. The JOSS paper should be little more than a brief summary of the software, how it fills a current niche and how it would be (or has been) used in research. At the very least, your section describing the contents of the documentation page ("Documentation and Tutorials") can be removed. Feel free to ask me any questions and keep me posted on your progress through these items.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2495647840/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

@Sierra-MC! :wave: I have a few small fixes ahead of starting the publication process. * It's exceptional to use **strong emphasis** in JOSS text, so please change "**data-driven development**" to "*data-driven* development". (Note that the emphasis is only on the development being *data-driven*.) * The Astropy project's [website advises](https://www.astropy.org/acknowledging.html) (as also indicated in their [CITATION.cff](https://citation-file-format.github.io/) file) the preferred citation being at least AstroPy [Paper III](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022ApJ...935..167A), if not also papers [I](http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2013A%26A...558A..33A) and [II](http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2018arXiv180102634T). We'd support you citing all three but you should at least cite Paper III instead of the GitHub repo. * Though the title of Kaufman et al. (2022) is all caps in the PDF of the proceedings, this appears (from the conference program) to be style, so the article title should be in sentence case (i.e. "How We’ll Know We Can Read All the Data in the PDS: A Testing Methodology for the Planetary Data Reader!!!"). * Rasterio also has a [recommended citation](https://github.com/rasterio/rasterio/blob/main/CITATION.txt) that is slightly different from what you've used in the paper. Once these are fixed we'll be ready to publish. Thanks!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2395164432/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I apologise for flagging this so late in the process but the paper is much too long. As mentioned in the docs on the [JOSS paper format](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/paper.html#what-should-my-paper-contain), > The paper should be between 250-1000 words. Authors submitting papers significantly longer than 1000 words may be asked to reduce the length of their paper. I believe the whole of the *Dependencies* section can be removed and, while I appreciate the spirit behind the *Author Contributions*, it's not typical in JOSS (and certainly not required). Note that the *Acknowledgements* are also counted so you may with to reduce those. The JOSS paper is also not intended to contain technical details beyond those relevant for a summary and statement of need. So it's good to point out that `py-ananke` is useful as a Python interface to `ananke` but probably not relevant that EnBiD and Galaxia are incorporated as git submodules. There are also at least two places (lines 35–39 and 50–57) where the list of references is unreasonably long. You should pare those don't to a few appropriate highlights. On more technical note, it looks like many of your references use the NASA ADS abbreviation codes (e.g. `\apj` for ApJ), which JOSS doesn't recognise, so the references are listed without journal names. Please replace these with the full journal names. If you want to check `@editorialbot`'s word count for the paper, you can comment here with `@editorialbot check repository` (as I've done [just above](https://github.com/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/6234#issuecomment-2395548795)).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2395557057/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

@doriannblain! Before I proceed with the publication process, I have some editorial comments that need addressing. The first two are minor: * Please expand the affiliation to use MPIA's full address (including "Germany" rather than "DE"). * The reference for Line et al. (2013) has ended up with an all-caps title, where it should probably be sentence case (which I think is AAS style). More substantially, the paper is too long. As mentioned in the docs on the [JOSS paper format](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/paper.html#what-should-my-paper-contain), > The paper should be between 250-1000 words. Authors submitting papers significantly longer than 1000 words may be asked to reduce the length of their paper. As a start, I think the *Workflows* section can be removed, as this isn't really the "high-level" functionality description we seek in the JOSS paper. I also think the table in the petitRADTRANS 3 update can be removed, though the prose after it seems relevant. If you want to check `@editorialbot`'s word count for the paper after editing it, you can comment here with `@editorialbot check repository`. I'll re-evaluate again after those changes and make further suggestions to reduce the paper length if necessary.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2395576323/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

@dominic-chang, I'm now getting around to this but there'll be a few more days of details before we're fully published. A few comments to start: * In the [Zenodo archive](https://zenodo.org/records/13936258), the right sidebar says "Version v1", rather than v0.3.1, which is what's tagged here and indicated in the actual zip file and tarball. Could you fix that v1? * Could add the country to the affiliations? "USA" will do. * You've added a footnote for the [Julia homepage](https://julialang.org). JOSS style is to rather [use inline hyperlinks](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/paper.html#links): > Open Journal publications are not limited by the constraints of print publications. We encourage authors to use hyperlinks for websites and other external resources. I'll take a closer look at the reference list in due course but note these two jumped out at me: * If I [follow the DOI](https://epubs.siam.org/doi/10.1137/141000671) for the *Bezanson et al. (2015)* reference for Julia, I can get a BibTeX entry that includes journal, volume and page number information, and is also a *2017* publication, not 2015. I'm not sure if you're using a different version of this entry? * The title of *Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration (2022)* has lost all capitalization. We use sentence case for reference titles in JOSS but you may need to protect e.g. `{Sagittarius} {A}* {Event Horizon Telescope}` and `{Milky Way}`. * *Johnson et al. (2020)* has some garbled character, presumably meant to be some form of apostrophe. If you happen to be able to fix those before I have a close look at the source, that'd be great, but I'll almost certainly end up opening a PR with other editorial fixes in the next few days.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2431200423/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

@ShaunFell, this has bubbled near the top of my list. My main concern is that the paper is very long by JOSS's standards. As indicated [in our docs](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/paper.html#what-should-my-paper-contain), > The paper should be between 250-1000 words. Authors submitting papers significantly longer than 1000 words may be asked to reduce the length of their paper. If we can at least get "closer" to 1000 words (I'll certainly allow 1250 and _maybe_ a bit more), I can have a closer look at the remaining text. My main feeling is that the "Key Features of GRBoondi" of section can be more or less removed or at least significantly shortened. For example, that you can use an arbitrary background has already been emphasised repeatedly in the text (e.g. second paragraph of "Generalised Proca" & much of the "Statement of Need"). Things like diagnostics, post-processing tools and boundary conditions are likely to change (or at least be extended) and are better off in docs. But if you can cut the paper by ~a quarter by other means, that's fine too. On a more technical note, the citation to the arXiv version of Coates & Ramazanoğlu (2022) can be replaced with the [published version](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022PhRvL.129o1103C).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2445506051/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I've created an [initial PR](https://github.com/ShaunFell/GRBoondi/pull/22) with a few small changes but the references generally need quite a lot of work. Please at least correct these to contain complete author lists, which should be available either from the relevant journal/archive website, or otherwise [NASA ADS](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/) is a good source for bibliographic information. If you haven't already, for software you should see if you can find a recommended citation through the relevant documentation but note that this can give mixed quality. Regarding the paper, note that the paper [shouldn't be much more than 1000 words](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/paper.html#what-should-my-paper-contain). It also [shouldn't contain API documentation](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/review_criteria.html#the-joss-paper), e.g., a list of functions, which I think would already save about 300 words. I had a quick go at running the Jupyter Notebook in `example_fexxv_doppler_shifts/Fit_real_line_shift_disc.ipynb` and immediately found I had to install `pyswarm`, which I couldn't find mentioned in the documentation. We [expect installation instructions to clearly explain any dependencies](https://joss.readthedocs.io/en/latest/review_criteria.html#installation-instructions). Python projects are usually provided as packages that can be installed with `pip`, though this can be directly from the repo with e.g. ``` $ pip install git+https://github.com/xragua/xraybinaryorbit ``` and doesn't have to be from PyPI. This also simplifies being able to import `xraybinaryorbit`. I then got as far as the "bounds input form" but had no idea what values to use. So I'd also suggest you check that all the examples run from a fresh checkout. I'd suggest moving all the `examples_...` folders to `examples/...` to keep the root folder a bit tidier. Ideally, you could use a documentation generation tool like [Sphinx](https://www.sphinx-doc.org) or [MkDocs](https://www.mkdocs.org/) and deploy docs to your personal GitHub page (e.g. `xragua.github.io/xraybinaryorbit`) or a third-party service like [Read the Docs](https://about.readthedocs.com/) but this isn't required. The API docs are usually then generated from docstrings. You _almost_ have docstrings, in that your functions print a help statement when they're executed. having a look over things but wanted to make a quick technical comment: > I also want first to test out the publication of ananke on the PyPI before proceeding with publishing to Conda Forge, or before looking into publishing the submodules EnBiD_ananke and Galaxia_ananke. We won't require you to create a Conda package because it looks like you can install `py-ananke` from `pip`, so Conda users can create a Conda environment and then use `pip`. I also don't think we need to wait for the PyPI registration because by configuring the repo as a package, it can be installed using the GitHub URL, as indicated in the instructions. I'll also note that I'm not sure you need the submodule trick any more either. Because `py-EnBiD-ananke` and `py-Galaxia-ananke` are themselves packages, I believe you could include them as dependencies like e.g. ``` py-EnBiD-ananke @ git+https://github.com/athob/py-Galaxia-ananke@0aac265 ``` though don't quote me on the syntax here! I don't expect this to happen now but you might want to use this in the future to simplify the build process. By the way, I'm having trouble installing `py-ananke` but I'll open an issue in the repo to resolve that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2347181538/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

What happened?

I've been working in a system where I occasionally read a multifile dataset and, in effect within the same Python process, subsequently want to write to some of the files containing that dataset. Instead, I get permission errors when I try to write to the file.

### What did you expect to happen?

I expected to not get a permission error.

### Minimal Complete Verifiable Example

```Python
import xarray as xr

ds = xr.Dataset(
    attrs={'blah': -1},
    coords=({'x': range(10)}),
    data_vars={'y': ('x', [10*i for i in range(10)])},
)

ds.isel(x=slice(0,5)).to_netcdf('file1.nc')
ds.isel(x=slice(5,10)).to_netcdf('file2.nc')

a = xr.open_mfdataset(paths=['file1.nc', 'file2.nc'])

ds.to_netcdf('file1.nc')
```

- [X] New issue — a search of GitHub Issues suggests this is not a duplicate.
- [X] Recent environment — the issue occurs with the latest version of xarray and its dependencies.

I.e., this code doesn't produce an error:
```py
import xarray as xr

ds = xr.Dataset(
    attrs={'blah': -1},
    coords=({'x': range(10)}),
    data_vars={'y': ('x', [10*i for i in range(10)])},
)

ds.isel(x=slice(0,5)).to_netcdf('file1.nc')
ds.isel(x=slice(5,10)).to_netcdf('file2.nc')

with xr.open_mfdataset(paths=['file1.nc', 'file2.nc']) as a:
    pass

ds.to_netcdf('file1.nc')
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/xarray/issues/comments/2359414273/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-09-24T21:42:37Z","updated_at":"2024-09-24T21:42:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

The docstring for `numpy.delete` specifues the type of the `obj` parameter as:
> **obj : _slice, int or array of ints_**

It seem it can also be anything that can be cast as an array of ints, including e.g. an iterator like `range(0,4,2)` or list of ints like `[0,2,3]`. I confirmed this by looking in the source and I think this line is where anything that isn't a slice, int or bool is cast as a `numpy.array`:

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/83bf22579b6eaa8113f6cff67b10fd9524965f23/numpy/lib/_function_base_impl.py#L5317

This type specification also doesn't mention that `obj` can be an array of booleans.

### Idea or request for content:

I'd open a PR but am not sure the best wording. I'm looking for something like

> **obj : _slice, int, array of bools or anything that can be cast as an array of ints_**

but that seems a bit verbose. Perhaps there's some NumPy jargon that's more succinct?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27459/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27459/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

I note that MOBSE is MIT-licensed, which appears consistent with TSE's licence. But MOBSE appears to be derived from (and indeed has a lot of identical code to) the original BSE, which as far as I can tell was (and still is) distributed without *any* licence. In it's current state, I don't think that part of the repo can be included in our review. There are theoretically two ways to resolve this. 1. The original author of BSE can be convinced to release it under an [OSI-approved licence](https://opensource.org/licenses) and, assuming that's the same version as used in MOBSE, MOBSE's licence can be made consistent. 2. You can remove MOBSE from the RSE repo and include its download as part of the TSE installation instructions. I think this boils down to adding `git clone https://gitlab.com/mobse/source-code mobse` before the existing instructions. The second option is reasonably straightforward and much more feasible than the first.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2268950198/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I've found two reviewers, so I'll start the proper review shortly but make a few remarks here. > Regarding your second comment about MOBSE: There are two minor changes I did to the mobse package in mobse/src/evolve.f and mobse/src/mobse.f to allow compatibility between my code and MOBSE, without actually changing the physics. Now I also highlight this in the README.md file. From that perspective would you think it is the most straightforward and user-friendly approach to only keep those two modified files in the repo but adding the rest of MOBSE with git clone ... like you suggested? One possibility is to include a patch file and instructions on how to apply it when installing it. You could possibly even bundle a script to download, patch and install MOBSE in the appopriate place. To be more concrete, here's how I started a patch file that I think has the desired effect. ``` $ git clone https://github.com/stegmaja/TSE $ git clone git@gitlab.com:mobse/source-code.git mobse $ cd TSE $ diff -ruN ../mobse/src mobse/src > test.patch $ cd ../mobse $ patch -p1 < ../TSE/test.patch patching file src/evolve.f patching file src/mobse.f ``` So you could include the equivalent of `test.patch` in this repo and apply it as part of the installation. It turns out you can also do something similar with `git`. In particular, replacing the last step with ``` $ git apply ../TSE/test.patch ``` worked for me. While experimenting, I also noticed that `mobse/input/const_mobse.h` also differ. I presume that doesn't matter. > The conda steps are to ensure that the user has all the required packages installed. That's fair though bear in mind that not all Python users have Conda, and the implication is that TSE requires Conda, which doesn't seem to be the case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2288262032/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

What happened?

I've been working in a system where I occasionally read a multifile dataset and, in effect within the same Python process, subsequently want to write to some of the files containing that dataset. Instead, I get permission errors when I try to write to the file.

### What did you expect to happen?

I expected to not get a permission error.

### Minimal Complete Verifiable Example

```Python
import xarray as xr

ds = xr.Dataset(
    attrs={'blah': -1},
    coords=({'x': range(10)}),
    data_vars={'y': ('x', [10*i for i in range(10)])},
)

ds.isel(x=slice(0,5)).to_netcdf('file1.nc')
ds.isel(x=slice(5,10)).to_netcdf('file2.nc')

a = xr.open_mfdataset(paths=['file1.nc', 'file2.nc'])

ds.to_netcdf('file1.nc')
```

- [X] New issue — a search of GitHub Issues suggests this is not a duplicate.
- [X] Recent environment — the issue occurs with the latest version of xarray and its dependencies.
